findings.reasoning: This page discusses the newly created 'Trump Account' (a tax-advantaged savings/investment account under OBBBA), 529 plans, and custodial accounts (UGMA/UTMA) as general educational content. It does not advertise a TurboTax-affiliated deposit product (e.g., a bank savings/checking account offered through a partner bank) that would require FDIC insurance disclosure. The Trump Account is invested in index funds/ETFs, not a deposit account, and no FDIC-insured bank deposit product is mentioned anywhere on the page. The strongest counter-reading is that 'savings account' language in the title could trigger deposit-product rules, but the article clarifies these are investment accounts (index funds/ETFs) rather than FDIC-insured bank deposits, and no bank partner or FDIC language appears at all. Therefore R-03's trigger condition (advertising a deposit product) is not met.

Rule R-03 applies only when a deposit product is being advertised and thus would require specific FDIC insurance wording. This article discusses a government-sponsored, tax-advantaged investment/savings vehicle called the Trump Account and compares it with 529 plans and custodial accounts. It does not advertise any Intuit/partner bank deposit product (such as a checking, savings, or prepaid deposit account), nor does it mention FDIC insurance at all. Therefore, the rule’s trigger condition is not met and the rule is not applicable to this page.

The article discusses a proposed government "Trump Account" that is an investment-style, tax-advantaged account invested in low-cost index funds/ETFs, not a bank deposit product. There is no advertising of a bank deposit product on this page, nor any FDIC-related language or a bank partner named. While the headline uses the phrase "savings account," the body clarifies the product is invested in mutual funds/ETFs and is not a deposit account. Therefore, the rule’s IF condition (deposit product advertised) is not met, so the rule is not applicable.
